narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and family background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 20, lower-middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1—and predicted a 69% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 academic decile, your LSAT score, and your family income bracket, ranked in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1, far below the typical student's average of around 5.5, was the strongest push toward the failure prediction, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers. Similarly, your LSAT score of 20—much lower than the dataset average of 37—reinforced this risk by indicating potential challenges with the rigorous reasoning skills needed for law school and the bar. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income actually nudged the prediction slightly toward success, as students from similar backgrounds sometimes show added resilience that helps them pass. Overall, your academic standings and test performance stood out as weaker than most applicants, tipping the scales despite these other strengths. While this decision stings, especially given your full-time commitment, it's not a final verdict on your potential as an Asian male pursuing law. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average levels, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your determination. Exploring part-time programs or community college pathways could also rebuild your academic decile profile. With targeted improvements, you can reapply stronger and show the model—and admissions teams—your true capabilities.
